text: In his Meditations, Marcus Aurelius returns again and again to a single corrective instruction: 'Confine yourself to the present.' This was not a call for impulsiveness but a structural claim about where value lives. The past is inaccessible; the future, uncertain. Only the present moment contains an action that can be chosen and executed. Yet Marcus noticed that the mind migrates habitually — into past grievances or future anxieties — and that each migration carries a small cost in attention and composure. Moment-to-moment vigilance meant catching these migrations early, before they compounded into what he called 'passion,' a state in which reason loses its footing.
question: According to the passage, what did Marcus Aurelius mean by 'confine yourself to the present'?

options.text: That the present moment is the only location where a choosable action exists
That one should ignore consequences and act impulsively
That memories and plans are inherently irrational
That composure requires suppressing all emotion
